results.possibleIdentity: Joseph L. Stern (c. 1920 – November 2004) was a prominent American television engineer and executive who spent nearly three decades at the Columbia Broadcasting System (CBS) in New York City. Beginning his career with CBS in 1946 after service in the Army Signal Corps and a stint at RCA, he rose to become the Vice President of Engineering for the CBS Television Services Division. During his tenure at CBS, he was instrumental in the development and implementation of color television systems and was responsible for the design and installation of major television transmitting facilities, including those at the Empire State Building and the initial planning for the World Trade Center's broadcast facility. In 1975, he founded Stern Telecommunications Corp. (STC), a New York-based consulting firm specializing in cable television (CATV), satellite, and microwave systems. Throughout his career, he was an active member of professional engineering societies and held multiple patents related to television technology and the integration of the internet with conventional broadcasting. He is remembered as a pioneer in the technical evolution of the television industry.

segment.text: Beginning his career with CBS in 1946 after service in the Army Signal Corps and a stint at RCA, he rose to become the Vice President of Engineering for the CBS Television Services Division.

During his tenure at CBS, he was instrumental in the development and implementation of color television systems and was responsible for the design and installation of major television transmitting facilities, including those at the Empire State Building and the initial planning for the World Trade Center's broadcast facility.
In 1975, he founded Stern Telecommunications Corp.
(STC), a New York-based consulting firm specializing in cable television (CATV), satellite, and microwave systems.
